critic_raw:
This transformer-based depression severity prediction design raises several fundamental methodological problems.

1. PHQ-9 LABEL IS SELF-REPORTED — NOT AN INDEPENDENT OUTCOME: The label is the most recently recorded PHQ-9 score within 30 days of a clinical encounter. PHQ-9 is a patient self-report instrument routinely collected during outpatient visits. The clinical notes (the primary transformer input) are written by clinicians who have often reviewed the PHQ-9 score before writing the note, or who document their interpretation of patient mood based on the score. The note therefore partially encodes the label through clinician documentation, creating a form of soft leakage where the input features and target are causally linked through clinical documentation practice, not independent prediction.

2. FINE-TUNING CLINICAL BERT ON NOTES WITH RMSE TARGET: The transformer is fine-tuned end-to-end with MSE loss on a continuous PHQ-9 target. Fine-tuning large pre-trained models (768-dimensional BERT) on 10,000 patients with a few years of notes is a high-dimensional fitting problem with substantial overfitting risk. The design uses early stopping on the validation set, but with a continuous regression target and a large pre-trained model, the validation loss surface can have multiple local minima, and the early stopping epoch selected may not generalize. The design does not report validation loss curves or check for overfitting through train/validation loss divergence.

3. PATIENT-LEVEL SPLIT VS. ENCOUNTER-LEVEL OUTCOME: The design assigns all records for a single patient to a single split based on the patient's first encounter. However, the prediction target is the most recently recorded PHQ-9 score, which for patients with multi-year records in the training window may be a score from year 3, while for test patients it may be from year 5. This creates an asymmetry in label timing relative to training that is not accounted for in the feature construction (notes over the 12 months prior to prediction date).

4. TRANSFORMER VS. RIDGE REGRESSION IS NOT AN ARCHITECTURE COMPARISON — IT IS A FEATURE COMPARISON: The transformer model uses clinical notes plus structured features; the ridge regression baseline uses structured features only and no notes. The stated hypothesis is that the transformer model outperforms traditional regression. But any difference could be entirely due to the addition of clinical notes as a modality, not the transformer architecture. A single-task linear model on clinical notes (e.g., TF-IDF + ridge regression) would disentangle the note contribution from the neural architecture contribution.
all_issues_raised: PHQ-9 label is routinely encoded in clinical notes that serve as model input, creating soft documentation leakage
Fine-tuning large BERT on 10,000 patients risks overfitting; validation early stopping may not generalize for continuous regression
Patient-level split assigns all records to one split but PHQ-9 label timing varies asymmetrically across splits
Baseline uses no notes, making the comparison a feature set comparison rather than an architecture comparison
PHQ-9 label is subject to informative missingness; exclusion of unobserved scores biases toward monitored high-severity subpopulation
Clinical notes contain prior PHQ-9 score mentions; transformer can learn to extract prior scores rather than predict from clinical observations
Patient split assignment for multi-year longitudinal patients is not specified; features spanning multiple years complicate temporal integrity
Bootstrap CI for RMSE difference may have incorrect coverage under right-skewed PHQ-9 error distributions; significance test assumptions not verified
Note extraction window includes same-encounter notes that may contain PHQ-9 scores, creating direct label leakage into transformer input
Patient-level temporal split is ambiguous when patients have multiple PHQ-9 assessments; feature extraction window for test-split patients may reach back into training-period data
End-to-end fine-tuning of 110M-parameter Clinical BERT on a small dataset risks catastrophic forgetting; no frozen-encoder ablation is included
Social determinants as features do not prevent the transformer from learning documentation-style proxies correlated with patient demographics
